items.title: The barcode project: a strategy to track the early naïve reservoir
items.summary: PI: Una T O'Doherty. Institution: EMORY UNIVERSITY. Award: $775,512. Memory CD4+ T cells are believed to be the primary reservoir for HIV; however, emerging evidence suggests an important role for naïve CD4+ T cells. Specifically, sequencing studies suggest the naïve reservoir contributes substantially to the intact HIV reservoir in chronic progressors, but is practically absent in elite controllers. While the naïve reservoir is small, it may have outsized effects on the overall reservoir since we find the naïve reservoir repopulates the memory based on preliminary clone tracking studies. Objective: The long-term goal is to dissect the role of the naïve reservoir on the overall reservoir in acutely infected individuals and in a primary model of latency. We will first determine the extent and frequency of naïve infection in acutely treated individuals. The n
items.why: HIV reservoir lab at Emory (O'Doherty) studying naïve CD4+ T cells in lymph node tissue; RNAscope required for viral RNA and cell-type marker colocalization without antibodies.

outreach_angle.hook: O'Doherty's lab is tracking HIV-infected naïve CD4+ T cells in lymph node tissue using molecular barcoding and clone tracking, a strategy that requires spatial validation of viral RNA and cell-state markers in situ. RNAscope is the ideal tool for localizing low-abundance HIV transcripts and confirming naïve T cell identity markers in tissue sections without relying on antibody availability.
outreach_angle.way_in: Validating which CD4+ T cells harbor intact viral RNA in lymph node tissue, and confirming the naïve phenotype of infected cells alongside viral detection, is critical for separating true naïve reservoir contributions from sequencing artifacts. RNAscope probes can multiplex HIV gag/pol RNA with naïve-state transcripts (e.g., CCR7, SELL) to map infection patterns directly in tissue.
outreach_angle.routing: West territory (SEA). Virology and immunology persona.
outreach_angle.draft_subject: HIV reservoir mapping in naïve CD4+ T cells
outreach_angle.draft_body: Dear Dr. O'Doherty, Congratulations on your NIH award to dissect the role of the naïve CD4+ T cell reservoir in HIV persistence. Your barcode-tracking approach is powerful for clonal dynamics, but spatially validating which naïve cells harbor intact viral RNA in lymph node tissue will strengthen your mechanistic claims. RNAscope in situ hybridization can simultaneously detect HIV transcripts and naïve-state markers (like CCR7 or SELL) in tissue sections, giving you the spatial and molecular resolution that sequencing alone cannot provide. We have established HIV and T cell marker probe sets and would welcome a brief conversation about how this might complement your tissue studies. Would you be open to a 20-minute call to discuss options?

items.title: Harnessing microglia-neuron communication to improve habituation in neurodevelopment disorders
items.summary: PI: Jose H Ledo. Institution: MEDICAL UNIVERSITY OF SOUTH CAROLINA. Award: $226,500. This research proposal explores the role of microglial cells in neurodevelopmental and neuropsychiatric disorders, particularly focusing on habituation deficits induced by maternal immune activation (MIA). Novelty habituation, a fundamental form of behavioral plasticity where the response to a repeated stimulus diminishes over time, is often disrupted in conditions like autism spectrum disorders (ASD). The underlying brain circuits and cellular mechanisms responsible for these deficits remain poorly understood, posing a significant barrier to developing effective therapeutic interventions. Our study hypothesizes that MIA, a model for prenatal infection, shifts microglial cells from a homeostatic to a reactive state, impairing their ability to regulate neuronal activity and thus disrupting
items.why: Neurodevelopmental psychiatry (autism) with microglia-neuron circuit mapping; needs in situ validation of microglial and neuronal markers in brain tissue.

outreach_angle.hook: Dr. Ledo's work on microglia-neuron communication in ASD requires mapping cellular states and circuit interactions in brain tissue, where in situ RNA detection is critical for validating microglial activation markers and neuronal response genes without relying on antibodies that may not capture functional state. RNAscope is a natural fit for spatially resolving these cell-cell interactions at the tissue level.
outreach_angle.way_in: Validating microglial state markers (homeostatic vs. reactive) and their correlation with neuronal activity markers in the same tissue section is challenging with standard immunofluorescence alone. RNAscope multiplex assays let you simultaneously detect microglial transcripts (e.g., activation genes) and neuronal markers (e.g., activity-dependent genes) with spatial resolution, confirming circuit dysfunction in your MIA model.

outreach_angle.draft_subject: In situ validation for microglia-neuron circuits in ASD models
outreach_angle.draft_body: Dr. Ledo, congratulations on your NIH award exploring how maternal immune activation disrupts microglia-neuron communication in autism-related habituation deficits. Validating microglial state transitions and their impact on neuronal circuits in tissue will be central to your work, and in situ RNA detection is often the most direct path to confirm functional states without antibody limitations. items.title: Epithelial Reprogramming Underpinning Euplastic versus Dysplastic Repair
items.summary: PI: Jaymin J Kathiriya. Institution: ICAHN SCHOOL OF MEDICINE AT MOUNT SINAI. Award: $873,801. PROJECT SUMMARY Lung possesses a remarkable ability to regenerate and restore normal lung function after severe acute injury. However, in the case of chronic injuries, the lung epithelial stem/progenitors fail to restore normal lung function and contribute to a pathologic regeneration, amounting to regenerative failure that typifies chronic restrictive lung diseases like Idiopathic Pulmonary Fibrosis (IPF). Both airway and alveolar epithelial stem cells are capable of differentiating into type 1 and type 2 alveolar epithelial cells (AT1 and AT2, respectively) via a series of intermediate cell states. items.title: Thyroid hormone mediated reprogramming of the basolateral amygdala by adolescent social deprivation
items.summary: PI: Deena M. Walker. Institution: OREGON HEALTH & SCIENCE UNIVERSITY. Award: $625,866. PROJECT SUMMARY Social disconnection in humans, particularly during adolescence, is associated with substance use disorder vulnerability. In mice, adolescent social isolation profoundly alters reward processing in adulthood; increasing the preference for drugs of abuse, enhancing associative learning, altering dopaminergic signaling and transcription within the BLA and extended reward circuitry. Intriguingly, these phenomena are all also associated with thyroid hormone signaling. Further, dysregulation of thyroid hormone in early life is associated lifetime risk for reward-related mood disorders and altered development of monoaminergic systems. In adults, thyroid hormone dysregulation is associated with substance- and alcohol use disorders. Therefore, we propose that the persistent impacts

items.title: Blood-Brain Barrier Repair in Alzheimer’s Disease with Epilepsy
items.summary: PI: Bjoern Bauer, Anika M.S. Hartz. Institution: UNIVERSITY OF KENTUCKY. Award: $719,390. More than 25% of patients with Alzheimer’s disease (AD) develop epilepsy as co-morbidity. In AD with epilepsy (ADxEpi), seizures accelerate cognitive decline and further reduce life expectancy compared to AD alone. One hallmark of both AD and epilepsy is blood-brain barrier dysfunction. We discovered that barrier dysfunction is more severe in ADxEpi patients compared to seizure-free AD patients. Collectively, our data suggest that a combination of Ab and seizure-released glutamate (Aβ/Glu) triggers a dual positive feedback loop which exac- erbates barrier dysfunction, seizures, and cognitive decline in ADxEpi. However, the detailed mechanism(s) that leads to barrier dysfunction in ADxEpi is/are unknown, and treatment options for ADxEpi patients are limited to anti-seizure drugs that by the

items.title: Liquidity of gene co-expression trajectories across the lifespan highlights delayed maturation and the perinatal GABA switch in schizophrenia risk
items.summary: Schizophrenia genetic and environmental risk factors play out largely in early life, biasing development toward a pathogenic trajectory that becomes clinically apparent in early adulthood, when the disorder typically onsets. Here, we convert snapshots of gene expression in postmortem brain at a moment in time into a dynamic lifetime series employing the "liquidity" metric, a novel tool to track the evolution of networks across time from a multi-systemic perspective. The landscape of normal prefrontal cortical development becomes increasingly "liquid" during the first two decades of post-natal life, with sharp discontinuities in known critical periods such as birth and adolescence. Neurotypical individuals free of apparent neuropathology with relatively elevated polygenic risk scores for schizophrenia exhibit a generalized delay in the dynamics of liquidity across these trajectories compared to below-average-risk individuals. Impacted biological processes strongly converge on delayed GABA-A receptor functional maturation, involved in establishing Excitatory/Inhibitory balance in brain during early development. Similar to patients with schizophrenia, neurotypical high-risk individual

outreach_angle.hook: This lab is mapping gene co-expression trajectories across human prefrontal cortex development and identifying GABA-A receptor maturation delays in schizophrenia risk individuals. They need spatial validation of GABA circuit markers and developmental gene signatures in postmortem brain tissue to confirm network-level findings at the cellular and regional level.
outreach_angle.way_in: Postmortem brain studies rely on dissociated tissue or bulk sequencing, which loses the spatial context of GABA maturation and E/I balance circuits across cortical layers and cell populations. RNAscope in situ hybridization lets them map GABA-A subunit expression, inhibitory interneuron markers, and co-expressed developmental transcripts directly in tissue sections, validating their liquidity trajectories with cellular and regional resolution.

items.title: Organization of Retinothalamic Circuits and their Contributions to Visual Processing in Primary Visual Cortex
items.summary: PI: EDWARD M CALLAWAY, Benjamin Knoell Stafford. Institution: SALK INSTITUTE FOR BIOLOGICAL STUDIES. Award: $475,000. Project Summary/Abstract The goal of this proposal is to characterize the connectivity and functional role of specific circuits in the mammalian visual system. This proposal focuses on neural circuits that transmit visual information from retinal ganglion cells (RGCs) in the retina, through different regions of the dorsal lateral geniculate nucleus (dLGN) in the thalamus, to specific populations of neurons in the primary visual cortex (V1). The combined activity of these visual pathway circuits results in the generation of tuning properties in V1 neurons (eg. direction selectivity) that make important contributions to visual perception and behavior. The objective of this proposal is to understand how specific retino-geniculo-cortical circuits, carrying different types of visual information

items.title: Schwann Cell Reprogramming after Nerve Injury
items.summary: PI: Rajeshwar B Awatramani, John P Svaren. Institution: UNIVERSITY OF WISCONSIN-MADISON. Award: $475,401. Abstract Myelination of axons in the nervous system is critical for not only conduction of action potentials, but also for providing tropic support to ensure long term survival of neurons in both the central and peripheral nervous systems. Myelin disorders are a major cause of neurological disease, and can be caused by genetic disorders, infectious disease, and inflammation. The peripheral nervous system has substantial plasticity in being able to regenerate after nerve injury, and critical transcription factors and their target gene networks have begun to be elucidated. Schwann cell reprogramming to a new differentiated state is a critical and rate limiting factor in peripheral nerve regeneration, particularly when regeneration is impaired as a function of aging/chronic denervation. There

We have recently provided insight into how recognition of commensal components by epithelial and innate immune cells triggers a specific immune reaction and why the nature of this reaction could foster host- microbe symbiosis instead of outright microbial rejection. items.title: Single-cell transcriptomics of heterogeneous patient-derived organoids reveals novel therapeutic targets in high-grade serous ovarian cancer
items.summary: High-grade serous ovarian carcinoma (HGSOC) is characterized by widespread peritoneal dissemination and poor long-term survival, largely driven by metastatic relapse following initial response to chemotherapy. Defining the molecular programs that enable tumor progression from the primary ovarian site to metastatic niches remains a key challenge. Here, we leverage patient-derived organoids (PDOs) coupled with single-cell RNA sequencing (scRNA-seq) to interrogate tumor evolution and identify regulators of metastatic competence in HGSOC. We profiled PDOs and matched formalin-fixed paraffin-embedded (FFPE) tumor samples from ovarian and omental disease sites across seven patients. Single-cell transcriptomic analysis revealed conserved and patient-specific cellular states and enabled reconstruction of inferred trajectories of tumor progression. Comparative trajectory analysis identified gene expression programs associated with metastatic transition from ovarian to omental tumors. Among these, the heparan sulfate proteoglycan AGRIN emerged as a consistently upregulated gene along the metastatic axis. Cell-cell communication analyses suggested that AGRIN-mediated signaling involves both e

items.title: Dissecting the sources of variation in neuronally differentiated iPSC lines through multi-omics analysis
items.summary: Induced pluripotent stem cells (iPSCs) are widely used as patient-specific disease models, yet substantial unexplained variability in molecular and functional readouts limits their reliability. Here, we systematically investigated the sources of variation in iPSC-derived neurons for three rare genetic disorders: Myotonic Dystrophy Type 1, chromodomain-DNA-helicase-binding protein 2-related disorder and N-acetylneuraminic acid synthase deficiency. This was performed by profiling multi-omics layers: genomics, epigenomics, transcriptomics, proteomics, metabolomics and lipidomics. Our study found that clonal variability was comparable to inter-patient differences and that neuronal differentiation state and nutrient-driven metabolic activity emerged as dominant contributors to variability observed across omics layers. Clonal differences could partly be attributed to stochastic differences in DNA methylation established during reprogramming. By modeling and correcting the observed variation, we improved the detection of disease-associated molecular signatures. Our study provides guidelines for improved study design and data analysis to minimize variability, enabling robust biomarker disc
items.why: iPSC-derived neuron work on rare genetic disorders needs in situ validation of lineage markers and disease-relevant targets; RNAscope ideal for confirming differentiation state and cell identity in 3D organoids.

outreach_angle.hook: Your lab is profiling iPSC-derived neurons across multiple omics layers to understand variability in disease models, but transcriptomic and proteomic data need spatial validation in differentiated neuronal tissue. RNAscope is the ideal tool to confirm neuronal differentiation state, validate cell-type identity, and localize disease-relevant transcripts (including rare genetic targets with limited antibodies) directly within your differentiated cultures or organoids.
outreach_angle.way_in: Multi-omics studies identify candidate biomarkers and disease signatures in iPSCs, but without in situ confirmation you cannot be certain expression patterns match the neuronal phenotype you've selected. RNAscope lets you map key lineage markers and disease-associated genes spatially across your differentiated populations, resolving which transcripts correlate with true neuronal identity and filtering noise from clonal or metabolic variation.

This removes a technical barrier (heating) and integrates HCR into a widely-used clinical histology workhorse, positioning it as a turnkey alternative to RNAscope for pathology labs.
impact_brief.why_it_matters: This is a direct competitive threat to RNAscope in the clinical and anatomical pathology segment. BOND-III is already installed in thousands of labs globally; coupling it with HCR eliminates friction for adoption and creates a platform-bundled competitor in a space where ACD has traditionally competed on technical merit alone. Molecular Instruments is moving from research positioning into clinical/clinical-adjacent workflows where simplicity and automation matter as much as performance.
at_risk: Clinical and hospital pathology labs evaluating in situ RNA detection for multiplex assays on their existing BOND-III infrastructure.
Market share in multiplexed RNA-ISH applications where labs prioritize automation convenience and single-vendor workflow integration over platform independence.
RNAscope's positioning as the 'gold standard' for ease of use if HCR on BOND-III reaches feature parity on speed and multiplexing depth.
holding_ground: RNAscope's single-molecule sensitivity and track record of specificity across diverse tissue types remain scientifically differentiated and have deep validation literature that HCR will take time to match.

ACD's platform-agnostic model and compatibility with multiple imaging systems, scanners, and workflows mean customers retain freedom; BOND-III integration locks users into Leica's ecosystem, which may repel labs seeking flexibility.

Ambient-temperature HCR on BOND-III solves a workflow convenience problem, but does not address whether HCR matches RNAscope's penetration in difficult tissues (FFPE, thick sections, high-background samples) or in high-plex panels where signal fidelity is critical.

items.title: Pulmonary vagal sensory neurons in chronic lung disease
items.summary: PI: Gary Charles Mouradian. Institution: MEDICAL COLLEGE OF WISCONSIN. Award: $467,332. PROJECT SUMMARY Preterm infants are born with immature lungs and immature neural control of breathing. They rely on supplemental oxygen therapy (hyperoxia; HX). Though necessary for survival, they require HX which progressively damages the lungs and causes bronchopulmonary dysplasia (BPD), the most common respiratory complication among preterm infants. Infants with BPD have worse neural control of breathing (nCOB) than age- matched non-BPD infants, suggesting HX dose and/or duration contribute to worse breathing control. With HX used to varying amounts and durations among nearly all preterm births and with BPD infants having a greater risk for adulthood respiratory health complications, understanding the dose- and duration-dependent effects of HX on breathing acutely and chronically remain
items.why: Pulmonary neurobiology lab studying vagal sensory circuits in chronic lung disease; needs in situ validation of neuronal subtypes and neuropeptide markers in lung tissue.

outreach_angle.hook: This lab is mapping how hyperoxia damages pulmonary vagal sensory circuits in bronchopulmonary dysplasia, a disease where understanding neuronal subtype composition and neuropeptide expression in lung tissue is critical to the mechanistic story. They are a strong fit for in situ RNA validation of vagal sensory neuron markers and neuropeptides in lung sections, where antibodies may be limited and spatial localization within the respiratory epithelium and neural plexuses matters.
outreach_angle.way_in: Validating vagal sensory neuron identity and neuropeptide expression (substance P, CGRP, VIP, etc.) in lung tissue requires methods that preserve spatial context and work in organs where neural markers can be sparse. RNAscope offers single-cell resolution mapping of these transcripts within lung architecture, without the cross-reactivity issues that plague immunohistochemistry for multiple neuropeptides.
outreach_angle.routing: MWA territory (West). Route to field rep covering Wisconsin / Milwaukee region.
outreach_angle.draft_subject: In situ neuropeptide mapping in pulmonary vagal circuits
outreach_angle.draft_body: Dr. Mouradian, your NIH-funded work on pulmonary vagal sensory neuron dysfunction in bronchopulmonary dysplasia caught our attention. Validating the identity and neuropeptide profiles of vagal neurons in lung tissue, especially across dose and duration of hyperoxia exposure, requires spatial resolution and specificity that traditional immunostaining often cannot deliver. RNAscope in situ hybridization excels at mapping low-abundance neuropeptide transcripts (substance P, CGRP, VIP, and others) at single-cell resolution within intact lung architecture. I'd like to discuss how our probe sets and multiplexing capability could accelerate your mechanistic work. Would you have 15 minutes for a brief call in the coming weeks?

This is the most direct competitive threat to RNAscope in the high-resolution, single-cell spatial market segment where customers are evaluating platform trade-offs.
at_risk: Xenium adoption could accelerate in academic and pharma labs if 10x leverages capital gains to undercut pricing or bundle spatial analysis with their established sequencing ecosystem.
RNAscope's platform-agnostic advantage may erode if 10x pursues aggressive bundling (instrument, reagents, software) that locks customers into their workflow despite higher total cost.
holding_ground: RNAscope's single-molecule sensitivity and validation depth across diverse tissue types remain differentiated; Xenium strength is primarily in throughput and automation, not sensitivity.

ACD's independence from sequencing or instrument hardware gives customers flexibility to combine RNAscope with any imaging platform, a structural advantage over 10x ecosystem lock-in that investor euphoria alone cannot erase.

This expands HCR's usability across a wider range of validated antibodies without requiring customers to source new reagents.
impact_brief.why_it_matters: Removing antibody compatibility friction is a direct competitive move against RNAscope's workflow simplicity and adoption curve. If HCR can credibly offer faster turnaround times and lower per-assay costs while now supporting existing antibody investments, it becomes a more compelling alternative for labs already embedded in Abcam reagent ecosystems, particularly in multiplex protein-RNA workflows.
at_risk: Price-sensitive customer segments in academia and contract research, especially those already committed to Abcam antibodies and seeking lower-cost alternatives to RNAscope
Speed-to-result positioning, if HCR's chemistry delivers materially faster assay protocols than current RNAscope workflows
Bundled ecosystem value proposition, since Abcam partnership signals a third-party validation layer that can reduce perceived technical risk in switching from RNAscope
holding_ground: RNAscope's single-molecule sensitivity and specificity remain unmatched in the literature; HCR compatibility with antibodies does not address whether HCR matches RNAscope's RNA detection floor
Deep, multi-tissue validation track record across disease models and tissue types; a new antibody partnership alone does not replace years of peer-reviewed performance data
Platform and instrument agnosticism: RNAscope works on any microscope, while HCR success still depends on customer adoption of Molecular Instruments hardware or compatible imaging systems

items.title: Integrated multi-omics identifies distinct macrophage alterations during progression of metabolic dysfunction-associated steatohepatitis - Nature
items.summary: Integrated multi-omics identifies distinct macrophage alterations during progression of metabolic dysfunction-associated steatohepatitis Nature
items.why: Bruker/NanoString CosMx spatial proteomics used for macrophage profiling in NASH; shows competitor deploying in fibrosis/TME niche adjacent to ACD's strength.

impact_brief.what_happened: Bruker/NanoString published a Nature paper using their CosMx spatial proteomics platform to profile macrophage dysfunction in NASH (metabolic dysfunction-associated steatohepatitis). This demonstrates their technology's capability in disease modeling and positions them as an active player in liver fibrosis and immune cell biology research.
impact_brief.why_it_matters: This publication signals Bruker/NanoString's investment in fibrosis and tumor microenvironment niches where ACD has strong footholds with RNAscope validation. Bruker's integrated proteomics angle (protein + spatial) and bundled ecosystem advantage could attract labs wanting single-vendor solutions for multi-omics workflows, especially in translational oncology and organ disease where immune profiling matters.
at_risk: Loss of design-in opportunities in fibrosis and TME research programs where labs now see CosMx as a complete protein+spatial option without need for orthogonal RNA ISH.
Bruker's ecosystem bundling (CosMx, Visium, instrument lock-in) may shift customer preference away from ACD's platform-agnostic model if labs prioritize workflow integration over flexibility.
Publication prestige in Nature validates CosMx for grant reviewers and journal editors, raising competitive bar for ACD validation studies in similar indications.
holding_ground: RNAscope remains the gold standard for single-molecule RNA sensitivity and specificity; CosMx is protein-focused and cannot replicate RNA isoform or low-abundance transcript detection ACD offers.

ACD's deep validation across diverse tissue types (liver, immune organs, TME) and independence from instrument vendors means customers retain flexibility to combine RNAscope with any imaging or analysis platform.

NASH and fibrosis research often requires both protein (immune markers) and RNA (metabolic, fibrotic gene expression) readouts; RNAscope + complementary protein tools may still be preferred to single-platform compromise.

The CEO emphasized MERFISH 2.0 capabilities, expanded ADC (antibody-drug conjugate) markers, and Lab Services offerings as part of a 'revamped' product and commercial strategy for 2026.
impact_brief.why_it_matters: Vizgen is reinforcing its position as a fully integrated multiplexed spatial platform with strengthened protein detection and co-detection workflows. This directly challenges RNAscope's traditional RNA-centric positioning and threatens to consolidate customers around a single instrument-plus-consumables ecosystem, which is the core bundling risk ACD faces.
at_risk: Customers seeking integrated RNA and protein co-detection in a single workflow may perceive MERSCOPE as more convenient than RNAscope plus separate protein assays or orthogonal platforms.
Vizgen's new capital and expanded Lab Services could accelerate adoption among early-stage researchers and translational programs that value turnkey service offerings.
RNAscope's platform-agnostic advantage may erode if Vizgen's MERFISH chemistry and protein capabilities reach feature parity with current RNAscope benchmarks.
holding_ground: RNAscope's single-molecule sensitivity and deep, multi-tissue validation remain differentiated and difficult to match quickly; chemistry alone does not replace years of clinical and research validation.
RNAscope's independence from any single instrument ecosystem gives customers flexibility to combine with their existing microscopy, flow, or sequencing infrastructure without ecosystem lock-in.

Vizgen's protein co-detection announcement lacks detail on performance metrics (sensitivity, specificity, multiplex ceiling); RNAscope can compete on data quality and specificity in head-to-head studies if Vizgen's protein assays have not closed the gap.

items.title: Molecular Instruments and Standard BioTools Announce Collaboration to Bring Amplified Multi-Omic Readouts to Imaging Mass Cytometry
items.summary: Molecular Instruments press release via newswire.
items.why: Molecular Instruments (HCR) partners with Standard BioTools (Hyperion IMC) to expand spatial protein-RNA multiplexing. Direct competitive threat to RNAscope positioning in spatial biology workflows.

impact_brief.what_happened: Molecular Instruments (HCR-based ISH) and Standard BioTools (Hyperion Imaging Mass Cytometry) have partnered to integrate amplified RNA detection with protein imaging on the same spatial platform. This creates a bundled multi-omic workflow combining HCR RNA readout with IMC protein detection in a single tissue section.
impact_brief.why_it_matters: This is a direct ecosystem play that couples HCR with a validated protein imaging instrument, addressing a key customer pain point (RNA and protein in one workflow without platform switching). It mirrors the bundling strategy that has worked for competitors like 10x and Bruker, and positions HCR as the RNA method of choice for IMC users, potentially locking customers into a complete Standard BioTools-Molecular Instruments stack rather than mixing and matching.
at_risk: RNAscope adoption in IMC labs and imaging mass cytometry facilities that value turnkey multi-omic protocols over best-of-breed flexibility.
Market perception that single-platform solutions (protein plus RNA from one vendor partnership) are easier to troubleshoot and support than integrating RNAscope into IMC workflows.
Sales traction in spatial biology labs already committed to Hyperion IMC, where HCR now becomes the path of least resistance for RNA.
holding_ground: RNAscope's single-molecule sensitivity and specificity advantage remain stronger than HCR for detecting low-abundance transcripts and validating results independently of amplification artifacts.
RNAscope's platform agnosticity means it is not locked into IMC, allowing customers to use RNAscope with fluorescence, immunofluorescence, and histology workflows simultaneously without vendor lock.

Deep validation in diverse tissue types and species gives RNAscope credibility as an independent reference standard, whereas bundled solutions may face adoption hesitation from labs seeking orthogonal RNA methods.

holding_ground: RNAscope's single-molecule sensitivity and proven validation across tissue types and pathology workflows remain the gold standard for research and diagnostic applications Vizgen does not yet dominate.
ACD's platform-agnostic positioning (no instrument lock-in) is differentiated from Vizgen's MERSCOPE hardware dependency; this flexibility appeals to labs with mixed instrument portfolios.

RNAscope's installed base and 15+ years of peer-reviewed precedent in ISH remain a structural moat against rapid displacement in established workflows, especially in clinical and translational pathology.

impact_brief.what_happened: Vizgen announced a spatial multiomics roadmap for AGBT 2026, including expansions of its MERSCOPE Ultra platform to organoid profiling, new modular panel customization (MERSCOPE Add-On Genes), upstream workflow improvements, cloud-based image analysis, and progress toward 3D thick-tissue imaging. The company is positioning organoid work as aligned with FDA and NIH interest in New Approach Methodologies (NAMs) as alternatives to animal models.
impact_brief.why_it_matters: Vizgen is actively deepening its platform's breadth and applicability in a research segment (organoids, NAMs) that overlaps significantly with RNAscope's customer base and regulatory tailwinds. The modular panel expansion and integrated workflow innovations reinforce Vizgen's ecosystem-bundling strategy, making MERSCOPE a more complete closed-loop offering. This move also signals Vizgen's confidence in competing on breadth of application rather than on single-molecule sensitivity alone, which is RNAscope's traditional differentiation.
at_risk: Organoid researchers may see MERSCOPE Ultra as a more purpose-built, vertically-integrated alternative if Vizgen's NAM positioning and workflow optimizations deliver on perceived ease-of-use and data integration.

Vizgen's Add-On Genes modular approach and cloud-based analysis lower the friction for hypothesis-driven hypothesis iteration, potentially reducing switching friction for labs already invested in MERSCOPE.

Regulatory and funding bodies championing NAMs (FDA, NIH) may view Vizgen's explicit alignment with organoid work as a credibility signal, creating momentum in a segment where ACD adoption is not yet as well-established.
holding_ground: RNAscope's single-molecule sensitivity and superior specificity remain unmatched, especially critical for low-abundance transcripts and crowded organoid microenvironments where signal-to-noise is paramount.

RNAscope's instrument-agnostic design and deep validation across diverse tissue and model systems (including organoids) offer researchers flexibility and reduce lock-in risk that Vizgen's integrated platform creates.
ACD has no inherent weakness in organoid applications; the gap is in go-to-market messaging and workflow integration, not in core technology or data quality.
